caption.text: In 1949, the West Broadway Housing Development would be constructed in Boston's South End, built on twelve city blocks bordered by D Street, B Street, West Seventh, and West Broadway. The development consisted of [972 units intended for white veteran families], and would replace the neighborhood shown on this 1919 atlas page. (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/D_Street_Projects)
caption.title: The West Broadway Neighborhood

caption.text: The development would undergo multiple renovations in the following decades to improve over-crowded living conditions, incorporate more green spaces, and introduce more community centers, improvements which have culminated in its current iteration, (West Broadway IV). The colored homes you see here were a part of the latest redevelopment, while most of the buildings are still traditional low-rise red-brick buildings. [https://www.bostonhousing.org/en/Departments/Planning-and-Real-Estate-Development/Mixed-Finance-Development/West-Broadway-Homes.aspx]
caption.title: Redevelopment

caption.text: The West Broadway Housing Development was one of the first public housing projects in Boston, and it was constructed in a political environment in which South Boston was viewed as a blighted neighborhood. This 1960 map shows the perceptions of South Boston through the middle of the twentieth century, deeming neighborhoods as either "deteriorating", "deteriorated", or "renewal area". The conditions depicted on this map are a far cry from what city planners had envisioned when the land which would become Boston's South End was first filled in.
caption.title: South End Renewal

caption.text: The tidal marshes south of Boston's downtown would be filled in through the first half of the 19th century, with the intention of providing homes for the city's wealthier residents. Yet the neighborhood's rapid industrialization, characterized by the expansion of wharves ...
caption.title: Making the South End

caption.text: ... and [the construction of railroads] would drive property values down, leading to a mass emigration of wealthy residents. The railroad junction shown here is just one block over from B Street, and the neighborhood's residents would grow well-acquainted with the shrill whistles of steam engines. So the question arises, who would now live in South Boston, and the West Broadway neighborhood in particular. (https://www.dorchesteratheneum.org/project/old-colony-railroad/)
caption.title: Making the South End

caption.text: The middle of the 19th century saw droves of Irish immigrants arriving in Boston, [spurred by the devastation of the Great Hunger], and the city would push these new arrivals into the South End. By 1882, the year this atlas page depicts, the rows of wooden tenement houses of the West Broadway neighborhood were filled Irish immigrants... (https://www.nps.gov/articles/000/jfk-and-the-history-of-irish-immigration-in-boston.htm)
caption.title: Early Arrivals

caption.text: ... many of whom would arrive at Constitution Wharf in Boston Harbor.
caption.title: Early Arrivals

caption.text: This 1857 drawing depicts Irish immigrants rejoining with family who had arrived in the prior decades, yet any excitement would soon be replaced by fear. Throughout the period of Irish immigration, they would be faced with immense hostility upon arrival to the predominately Protestant city, not only due to their Catholic faith...
caption.title: Early Perceptions

caption.text: ... but the [typhus] which many had contracted on the over-crowded ships which had brought them to Boston. City officials would keep meticulous records of those who had contracted the disease. (https://irishboston.org/2022/on-june-1-1847-six-year-old-irish-girl-is-the-first-to-die-at-bostons-deer-island-quarantine-hospital/)
caption.title: Early Perceptions

caption.text: The city would [build a quarantine hospital on Deer Island] about eight miles from downtown Boston, shown on this 1882 atlas just off the tip of Point Shirley. (https://greatfaminevoices.ie/boston/the-history-of-deer-island-famine-irish-quarantine-station-in-boston/)
caption.title: Diseased Perceptions and Quarantine

caption.text: This image from 1847 depicts the women's quarter of the hospital. Hundreds would never leave Deer Island, and those who would still carried diseased perceptions in the eyes of the city's residents.
caption.title: Diseased Perceptions and Quarantine

caption.text: Those who survived initial arrival to the city had little capital, and city officials push ed them into the lower-class neighborhoods of South Boston, this West Broadway neighborhood included.
caption.title: Forming Community

caption.text: Residents of this neighborhood would often work as unskilled laborers on the wharves just north of their new home...
caption.title: Finding Work

caption.text: ... often working as clam diggers, as shown here in this 1882 photograph, construction workers, and any other occupations deemed undesirable by the local population. Irish women would often find work as domestic servants.
caption.title: Finding Work

caption.text: [The parish of Our Lady of the Holy Rosary (OLHR) would be constructed in 1884] during a wave of efforts by the Diocese of Boston to support the city's growing, yet still neglected Catholic population. It would become a pillar of West Broadway's Irish community. (https://dbnews.americanancestors.org/2018/11/28/our-lady-of-the-holy-rosary-south-boston-records-now-browsable/#:~:text=Latest:-,Our%20Lady%20of%20the%20Holy%20Rosary%20(South%20Boston)%20records%20now,Boston)%20Marriages%2C%201884%2D1900)
caption.title: Catholic Roots

caption.text: Along with the parish's initial construction, this rectory was built to provide housing for the parish priest.
caption.title: Catholic Roots

caption.text: OLHR was a smaller parish which experienced little growth over its fifty-six year existence, indicative of the lack of development the West Broadway neighborhood experienced before the land clearing in 1941.
caption.title: Forming Community

caption.text: The parish's founding pastor, John J. McNulty lived in the parish's rectory seen in next to OLHR, as would the church's consequent pastors who both lived in the neighborhood and served its spiritual and communal needs.
caption.title: Forming Community

caption.text: A majority of the residences in the West Broadway neighborhood were inhabited by families, and schools were quickly built to serve the educational needs of the neighborhood's children.
caption.title: Forming Community

caption.text: The Norcross School, shown here on the corner of West Fifth and D Street, was opened in 1868 as an all-girls school...
caption.title: Communal Education

caption.text: ... and through the turn of the 20th century, its student body was representative of the neighborhood's dominant Irish Catholic Population. This photograph was captured in the 1890s, showing Ms. Lewis' classroom.
caption.title: Communal Education

caption.text: Annie Duran lived at this location, 121 D Street. 1890s baptismal records show that she was baptized at Our Lady of the Holy Rosary in 1894, and as an Irish-Catholic resident of this neighborhood, she would have likely attended the Norcross School for Girls. Annie would marry Martin Mulkern at OLHR in 1919.
caption.title: The Residents

caption.text: Annie Duran's family name is not listed on the building in which she lived, and through cross-referencing the sacramental records of Our Lady of the Holy Rosary, none of the church's parishioners who lived in this neighborhood have their names listed on this 1919 atlas page. This is indicative of the working-class, borderline impoverished economic condition of the area... its residents were renters, not owners.
caption.title: The Residents

caption.text: The 20th century brought little material change to the buildings of this neighborhood, and Catholicism to be an integral component of this community. [The Roman Catholic Church's Ne Temere decree in 1908] is noted here on the first page of the OLHR' marriage registry, establishing new rules for inter-denominational marriage. (https://www.ewtn.com/catholicism/library/decree-on-betrothal-and-marriage-2299)
caption.title: Marriage at Our Lady of the Holy Rosary

caption.text: The church would require dispensation letters for these occurrences, an example of which is seen here, written in 1929 by a Protestant man named Alfred, who promises to respect his soon-to-be wife's Catholic faith, and to raise their children in the Catholic religion.
caption.title: Marriage at Our Lady of the Holy Rosary

caption.text: Ellen T. Feeney, the Catholic woman Alfred was marrying lived at this location, 58 West 6th Street, yet it is unclear if the newlyweds remained in the neighborhood after their marriage at OLHR in 1929.
caption.title: Marriage at Our Lady of the Holy Rosary

caption.text: On the same block as Ellen T. Feeney's pre-marital residence stands St. Peter Lithuanian Church. While the Irish were the first to settle in the West Broadway neighborhood, Catholic Lithuanian immigrants would begin to arrive at the end of the 19th century, spurred by political oppression under the Russian Empire. Just as OLHR had been built to serve the needs of the neighborhood's Irish community, this church was opened to support the growing Lithuanian population.
caption.title: Sharing the Neighborhood

caption.text:
Facing Flaherty Way and rows of tenement houses, [this parish was consecrated in 1904] ... 
(https://www.ewtn.com/catholicism/library/decree-on-betrothal-and-marriage-2299)
caption.title: Sharing the Neighborhood

caption.text: ... and remains an enduring symbol of faith in for the city's Lithuanian residents.
caption.title: Sharing the Neighborhood

caption.text: In the decades before the 1941 land clearing, the multiethnic Catholic community had solidified the identity of this neighborhood, yet the lack of upward mobility faced by its immigrant population was reflected through the condition of the homes in which they lived, as the wooden homes were falling into disrepair, as seen in this 1924 photograph of Gold Street. Residents who did manage to break through the constraints of poverty would move out of the neighborhood ...
caption.title: A "Blighted" Community

caption.text: ... leaving dilapidated homes behind.  This 1940 photograph of a residence on West Seventh Street depicts a scene which would become commonplace, as the neighborhood's lower-class residents could not fund renovation, and the city had a vision of the neighborhood which cared little about home improvement.
caption.title: A "Blighted" Community

caption.text:
Boston's [rapidly growing population in the first half of the 20th century dramatically increased housing demands]. City planners began using the terms "slum" and "blighted" to describe low-income residential neighborhoods in an attempt to justify land clearing. The West Broadway neighborhood, shown in this 1926 arial image of South Boston, would be put within the city's crosshairs. 
(https://globalboston.bc.edu/index.php/home/eras-of-migration/second-wave/)
caption.title: The Beginning of the End

caption.text: The official land clearing of the neighborhood was announced in the late 1930s, and active demolition commenced in 1941. The project would be stalled after the the U.S joined World War II, yet the stall would not save the neighborhood's wooden residential homes.
caption.title: The Beginning of the End

caption.text: This photograph of Gold Street was taken in 1940, a year before it would be demolished. Yet it was not only 19th century wooden homes which were wiped out in the land clearance, but Our Lady of the Holy Rosary as well. In its place today stands one of the many red-brick public housing units of the West Broadway IV development.
caption.title: The Beginning of the End

caption.text: By chance, St. Peter Church would survive the clearance. When construction of the West Broadway Housing Development was completed in 1949, the church would be one of the few buildings which remained from the pre-development neighborhood. The photograph here depicts the housing development in the first decades after construction, with St. Peter Church now peering over rows of brick apartments, the wood tenements of Flaherty Way merely a memory of a neighborhood of the past.
caption.title: Lingering Memories
